Reviews

M
Customers AI · Reviews
High confidence500 reviewsLast sync 6m ago
The tail costs
A 4.7 average hides a costly tail
The headline looks healthy, but 1–2★ customers repeat at 7% versus 52% for 5★ — and 22 of those complaints are still unanswered. The score is fine; the money leak underneath it isn't.
Root cause · Ops
60% of bad reviews are one broken process
Six in ten negative reviews trace to late delivery. This isn't a sentiment problem you fix with replies — it's a dispatch problem. The lever is in the Operations room, not here.
Recovery
A resolved complaint is worth five unanswered ones
Customers whose complaint was resolved come back at 41%; unresolved, 8%. With 22 sitting unanswered, roughly $95K of lifetime value is recoverable just by replying — ranked, below.
1 Rating & loyalty — does a star rating translate into customers who come back?
Repeat rate and lifetime value by star rating
Bars = repeat rate · line = average LTV · the gradient is the point
A rating isn’t sentiment — it’s a forecast of behaviour. A 5★ customer comes back more than half the time and is worth $4,200; a 1★ customer comes back 6% of the time and is worth $480. Both repeat rate and lifetime value collapse together as the rating falls. The good news is most reviews sit on the right; the cost is concentrated in a small, expensive tail on the left.
2 Root cause — why the unhappy ones are unhappy, traced to the process behind it
30 negative reviews (1–3★) · $130K LTV at risk · ranked by value at stake, each mapped to the room that owns the fix
1
Late delivery
18 reviews · 60% of negatives
→ Operations · Dispatch
$72K
LTV at risk
2
Damaged or wrong item
6 reviews · 20%
→ Operations · Fulfilment
$28K
LTV at risk
3
Product quality
3 reviews · 10%
→ Operations · Production
$16K
LTV at risk
4
Communication / support
2 reviews · 7%
→ Customers · Support
$9K
LTV at risk
5
Other
1 review · 3%
—
$4K
LTV at risk
Most of the damage is operational, not emotional. Six in ten negative reviews are late deliveries — a dispatch problem, fixable in Operations, not by answering reviews more nicely. Treating reviews as a customer-service queue misses this; treating them as a defect log points you straight at the process to fix.
3 Recovery — does answering an unhappy customer actually win them back?
Complaint resolved · 8 reviews
Repeat rate
41%
Avg response
4 hrs
LTV retained
$34K
Left unanswered · 22 reviews
Repeat rate
8%
Avg age
11 days
LTV at risk
$95K
Answering works: a resolved complaint repeats at 41% against 8% unanswered — five times the recovery. Right now 22 sit unanswered for an average of 11 days, with about $95K of lifetime value attached. Every day one stays open, the chance of winning that customer back drops. This is the cheapest recovery lever you have.
4 At-risk reviews — who to answer first, ranked by the lifetime value on the line
22 unanswered · $95K recoverable · ranked by lifetime value at risk, not by recency
★★★★★
Boonchai K.
“Flowers arrived wilted and three hours late.”
Late delivery
$8.2K
LTV at risk
Recover

Wrong item
$4.1K

Recover

“The cake was damaged by the time it arrived.”

$3.6K
LTV at risk
Recover

“Driver couldn't find the address and gave up.”
Late delivery
$2.9K

Recover
★★★★★
Arun S.
“Lovely, but smaller than the photo suggested.”
Quality
$1.8K
LTV at risk

★★★★★
Nicha W.
“Promised morning delivery, came after dark.”
Late delivery
$1.5K
LTV at risk
Recover
The top of this list is where the money is — a handful of high-value customers whose relationship is recoverable if you reach them now. Answer these before the rest, route the late-delivery cases to Operations as defects, and send the batch to the Bridge to draft responses.

A review score on its own is a vanity metric — it goes up, everyone nods, nothing changes. Tied to repeat rate and lifetime value, it becomes something useful: an early-warning signal for churn and a defect log pointing at the process that caused it. The question this room answers isn’t “are we liked” — it’s “who are we losing, why, and is it worth fixing.”

Ask Magellan anything…
